content.text: Vault Integration in CI/CD Pipelines

Storing secrets as static GitHub Actions secrets or environment variables is the most common mistake in GenAI CI/CD pipelines. Static secrets can't be audited per-access, persist indefinitely, and are copied into every environment that needs them. HashiCorp Vault — integrated via OIDC — eliminates static secrets entirely: each CI job authenticates with its own short-lived JWT, fetches exactly the secrets its policy permits, and those credentials expire when the job ends.

This document covers the full integration pattern: Vault OIDC auth for GitHub Actions, policy design, secret path conventions for GenAI workloads, the ExternalSecret Operator for Kubernetes runtime delivery, and dynamic secrets for databases and cloud credentials.

attrs.alt: Vault integration architecture across CI runner, Vault, secret paths, and Kubernetes runtime

content.text: OIDC Authentication: No Static Vault Tokens in CI

GitHub Actions natively supports OIDC token issuance. Each job receives a signed JWT asserting the repository, branch, workflow name, and environment. Vault's JWT auth method verifies this token against GitHub's JWKS endpoint and issues a short-lived Vault token scoped to a policy.

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml (relevant auth section)
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write   # required for OIDC token issuance
      contents: read

    steps:
      - name: Authenticate to Vault via OIDC
        id: vault-auth
        uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v2
        with:
          url: https://vault.internal:8200
          method: jwt
          role: genai-deploy          # Vault role name
          secrets: |
            secret/data/genai/prod/openai/api-key value | OPENAI_API_KEY;
            secret/data/genai/prod/anthropic/api-key value | ANTHROPIC_API_KEY;
            secret/data/genai/ci/container-registry password | REGISTRY_PASSWORD;

      # Secrets are now available as masked env vars
      - name: Build and push image
        run: |
          echo "$REGISTRY_PASSWORD" | docker login registry.internal -u ci --password-stdin
          docker build -t registry.internal/genai-service:$GITHUB_SHA .
          docker push registry.internal/genai-service:$GITHUB_SHA

      - name: Run integration tests
        env:
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ env.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
        run: pytest tests/integration/ -x
The vault-action step automatically masks secret values in logs. The Vault token it uses has TTL=1h and is revoked at job end — nothing persists between runs.
Vault Role and Policy Configuration

A Vault role binds an OIDC claim set (repository, branch, environment) to a policy. For GenAI services, define separate roles for CI (read staging secrets) and deploy (read prod secrets), and never grant CI jobs prod access unless deploying.

# Register JWT auth roles in Vault (run once by platform team)
vault write auth/jwt/role/genai-ci \
    role_type="jwt" \
    bound_audiences="https://github.com/org/genai-service" \
    user_claim="sub" \
    bound_claims_type="glob" \
    bound_claims='{
      "sub": "repo:org/genai-service:*",
      "ref_type": "branch"
    }' \
    policies="genai-ci" \
    ttl="1h"

vault write auth/jwt/role/genai-deploy \
    role_type="jwt" \
    bound_audiences="https://github.com/org/genai-service" \
    user_claim="sub" \
    bound_claims_type="glob" \
    bound_claims='{
      "sub": "repo:org/genai-service:ref:refs/heads/main",
      "environment": "production"
    }' \
    policies="genai-deploy" \
    ttl="1h"
Secret Path Conventions for GenAI Workloads

Consistent path naming prevents secrets from ending up in the wrong environment. The convention `secret/genai/<env>/<provider>/<key-name>` encodes environment and provider into the path, making policy rules simple and readable.
Path
Contents
Who Reads It
secret/genai/prod/openai/api-key
Production OpenAI key (value, key_id, rotated_at)
Deploy job, ESO in prod namespace
secret/genai/staging/openai/api-key
Staging OpenAI key (lower rate limit tier)
CI job, ESO in staging namespace
secret/genai/prod/anthropic/api-key
Production Anthropic key
Deploy job, ESO in prod namespace
secret/genai/ci/container-registry
Registry push credentials
CI build job only
secret/genai/prod/db/postgres
GenAI service DB credentials
ESO in prod, NOT CI
aws/creds/genai-deploy-role
Dynamic AWS STS credentials (TTL=15min)
Deploy job only
ExternalSecret Operator for Kubernetes Runtime

In production, pods read secrets via Kubernetes environment variables — they never call Vault directly. The ExternalSecret Operator (ESO) runs as a cluster controller and keeps Kubernetes Secrets synchronized with Vault on a configurable refresh interval.

# k8s/genai-service/external-secret.yaml
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: genai-api-keys
  namespace: genai-prod
spec:
  refreshInterval: 60s       # re-sync from Vault every 60 seconds
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-backend
    kind: ClusterSecretStore
  target:
    name: genai-api-keys     # resulting K8s Secret name
    creationPolicy: Owner
  data:
    - secretKey: OPENAI_API_KEY        # env var name in the pod
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/genai/prod/openai/api-key
        property: value

    - secretKey: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/genai/prod/anthropic/api-key
        property: value

# k8s/genai-service/deployment.yaml (secret reference, no values)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: genai-service
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: genai-service
          image: registry.internal/genai-service:latest
          envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: genai-api-keys   # managed by ESO, never hardcoded

When Vault is updated (e.g., after key rotation), ESO detects the new secret version within 60 seconds and updates the Kubernetes Secret. Pods pick up the new value on the next env read — or immediately if the application uses file-based secret mounting with inotify.
Dynamic Secrets for Database Credentials

LLM services often need database access (for storing eval results, conversation history, or vector embeddings). Instead of rotating static DB passwords, use Vault's database secrets engine to generate short-lived credentials per workload.

# Configure Vault database secrets engine (platform team, run once)
vault secrets enable database

vault write database/config/genai-postgres \
    plugin_name=postgresql-database-plugin \
    allowed_roles="genai-service" \
    connection_url="postgresql://{{username}}:{{password}}@postgres.internal:5432/genai" \
    username="vault-admin" \
    password="$VAULT_ADMIN_PG_PASSWORD"

vault write database/roles/genai-service \
    db_name=genai-postgres \
    creation_statements="CREATE ROLE '{{name}}' WITH LOGIN PASSWORD '{{password}}' VALID UNTIL '{{expiration}}'; GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO '{{name}}';" \
    default_ttl="1h" \
    max_ttl="24h"

# app/db.py — fetch dynamic credentials at startup
import os, hvac, sqlalchemy

def get_db_engine() -> sqlalchemy.Engine:
    vault_addr = os.environ["VAULT_ADDR"]
    vault_token = os.environ["VAULT_TOKEN"]   # injected by ESO or workload identity

    client = hvac.Client(url=vault_addr, token=vault_token)
    creds = client.secrets.database.generate_credentials(name="genai-service")
    username = creds["data"]["username"]
    password = creds["data"]["password"]

    return sqlalchemy.create_engine(
        f"postgresql+psycopg2://{username}:{password}@postgres.internal:5432/genai",
        pool_pre_ping=True,
        pool_recycle=3600,   # refresh connections before lease TTL
    )
Dynamic DB credentials mean that even if a credential is captured in a memory dump or log, it expires in 1 hour and can never be reused for a new connection after TTL.
Auditing Secret Access

Every secret read in Vault generates an audit log entry. Configure Vault's file audit device and ship logs to your SIEM. For GenAI services, alert on: reads from prod paths by CI jobs (policy misconfiguration), reads outside business hours (potential compromise), and reads for keys that have already been rotated.

# Enable file audit log (write to stdout for log aggregator pickup)
vault audit enable file file_path=stdout

# Sample audit log entry (JSON, one per line)
# {
#   "time": "2024-01-15T10:23:41Z",
#   "type": "response",
#   "auth": {
#     "client_token": "hmac-sha256:...",
#     "accessor": "hmac-sha256:...",
#     "display_name": "jwt-repo:org/genai-service:ref:refs/heads/main",
#     "policies": ["genai-deploy"],
#     "token_type": "service"
#   },
#   "request": {
#     "operation": "read",
#     "path": "secret/data/genai/prod/openai/api-key",
#     "remote_address": "10.0.1.42"
#   },
#   "response": { "data": { "data": "<hmac-redacted>" } }
# }
Least-Privilege Access for Eval Pipelines

Automated eval pipelines present a unique challenge: they need LLM API keys to call models, but they run on shared CI infrastructure and process potentially sensitive prompt datasets. Apply a separate Vault role for eval jobs that grants access only to lower-rate-limit evaluation keys, never the production keys used in the live service.

# .github/workflows/eval.yml (eval-specific Vault role)
jobs:
  eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
    steps:
      - uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v2
        with:
          url: https://vault.internal:8200
          method: jwt
          role: genai-eval          # lower-privilege role
          secrets: |
            secret/data/genai/eval/openai/api-key value | OPENAI_API_KEY;
            secret/data/genai/eval/anthropic/api-key value | ANTHROPIC_API_KEY;

      - name: Run eval suite
        run: python3 scripts/run_evals.py --suite regression --parallel 4
Eval keys can have daily spend caps set at the provider level (e.g., $50/day). Even if an eval pipeline is misconfigured and runs in a loop, the blast radius is bounded.
Local Development Without Vault

Developers running the service locally should not have Vault access in the same way CI does. Provide a developer workflow that uses a local .env file (gitignored) with sandbox/development-tier keys, and a setup script that fetches them from Vault when the developer has authenticated interactively.

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/dev-secrets.sh
# Fetch development-tier secrets from Vault for local use.
# Requires: vault login (interactive, cached in ~/.vault-token)

set -euo pipefail

VAULT_ADDR="${VAULT_ADDR:-https://vault.internal:8200}"
ENV_FILE=".env.local"

echo "Fetching dev secrets from Vault..."

OPENAI_KEY=$(vault kv get -field=value secret/genai/dev/openai/api-key)
ANTHROPIC_KEY=$(vault kv get -field=value secret/genai/dev/anthropic/api-key)
PG_URL=$(vault kv get -field=url secret/genai/dev/db/postgres)

cat > "$ENV_FILE" << EOF
# Generated by scripts/dev-secrets.sh — do not commit
OPENAI_API_KEY=${OPENAI_KEY}
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=${ANTHROPIC_KEY}
DATABASE_URL=${PG_URL}
EOF

echo "Written to $ENV_FILE"
echo "Run: set -a && source $ENV_FILE && set +a"

# .gitignore additions
.env.local
.env.dev
.env*.local
*.vault-token
Summary
Use Vault JWT/OIDC auth so CI jobs authenticate with their ephemeral GitHub OIDC token — no static Vault tokens stored in GitHub Secrets.
Define separate Vault roles for CI (staging read) and deploy (prod read + dynamic AWS creds); encode environment into policy path patterns.
Follow the path convention `secret/genai/<env>/<provider>/<key>` to make policies readable and prevent cross-environment leakage.
Deploy ExternalSecret Operator to sync Vault secrets into Kubernetes Secrets automatically (60s refresh); pods read via envFrom, never calling Vault directly.
Use Vault's database secrets engine to issue short-lived DB credentials (TTL=1h) instead of static passwords — eliminates a major credential rotation burden.
Enable Vault audit logging and ship to a SIEM; alert on prod path reads from CI roles or reads on already-rotated key versions.
